data.title: Booking Holdings: Travel Is Slowing Down And Will Crush This Stock's Premium (Downgrade)
data.description:
For years, Booking Holdings (NASDAQ:BKNG) has proven itself to be an outlier and rewarded investors who stayed in for the long haul. The world's largest online travel agency (OTA) has outperformed both of its key peers, Expedia (EXPE) and Airbnb (ABNB) this year, as its bookings growth rates surpassed the competition through the company's aggressive supply expansion.

Now, however, as Booking.com is sitting on ~15% gains since January and ~45% gains over the past twelve months, the key question for investors is: can Booking.com truly justify its valuation premium when it's subject to the exact same travel headwinds as the rest of its peers?

I last wrote a buy opinion on Booking in March, when the stock was trading in the low $4,000s. I've found Booking to be a great stock to trade in the short to medium term: but now, amid sharp compression in its bookings growth rates and earnings multiples that dwarf its rival Expedia, I'm far less optimistic on Booking.com's prospects. While I still view this company to be the highest-quality investment in the hotel/travel space, I believe we're facing at least a year of macro headwinds that will slow demand and take momentum out of this stock's sails. As such, I'm downgrading Booking to a sell rating.

To me, these are the core reasons to be concerned on Booking.com:

  • Very choppy bookings trends: Even before macro headwinds kicked in, Booking Holdings exhibited very choppy quarterly bookings trends. In the most recent quarter, Booking's pace of gross bookings growth was cut in half. At the same time, Booking has maintained its valuation premium: which in my view should be reserved for companies whose growth trends are more consistent and reliable.
  • Unique and sharp travel headwinds: A number of factors are threatening to hold back travel this year, particularly for U.S. consumers. A potential recession is cutting into consumer spending, and travel is one of the biggest discretionary budget items that can be easily slashed. On top of this, a weaker U.S. dollar is discouraging travel to certain markets: for example, the euro is now sitting at $1.14, which is ~10% higher than at the start of the year: a major bite to any travel budget for an outbound European vacation. At the same time, some of President Trump's rhetoric is discouraging international travel into the U.S. - so the U.S. may not necessarily see the same inbound travel benefits from the weaker dollar.
  • Performance no longer materially above peers: A chunk of Booking.com's historical premium owed to its reliable gross bookings/revenue growth premiums over its peers, notably Expedia. Now, Booking.com's growth is only a few points higher than Expedia, and roughly in-line with Airbnb.

All of this is against the backdrop of incredibly expensive valuation multiples for Booking. Booking's 20x forward adjusted EBITDA multiple is ~10% richer than Airbnb and more than double Expedia's multiple:

As such, I'm no longer comfortable holding onto my Booking position at this juncture. I'm content to lock in gains here and move to the sidelines until Booking's multiples compress back down.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Booking's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Booking.com's revenue grew 8% y/y to $4.76 billion, outpacing Wall Street's expectations of $4.59 billion (+4% y/y) by a four-point margin. Despite the wide revenue beat, we do note that Booking.com's revenue growth decelerated quite sharply from 14% y/y growth in Q4.

Equally concerning was the wide deceleration we observed in bookings. As shown in the chart below, Booking's gross bookings growth slowed to 7% y/y, less than half the growth pace relative to 17% y/y growth in Q4. As a reminder here, Booking recognizes bookings when customers book their trips/place their reservations, but revenue is recognized when trips are actually taken (when hotel stays are completed or flight segments are flown).

As such, Booking's slowdown in bookings growth is a dire forward-looking indicator for the key summer travel season. Now, it is possible that bookings lead times are shortening (an excuse that Airbnb has used in the past to defend slower bookings growth) - that is, people may be making more last-minute trip decisions. This may be an especially reasonable situation amid a recession, if people are unsure about the status of their jobs (or stock portfolios), or about currency weakness when traveling to foreign destinations. It is possible that more travelers will be booking closer to their travel dates for the summer travel season.

Booking's own guidance calls for a re-acceleration in bookings growth to 10-12% y/y in Q2, which is potentially a hopeful signal that many customers that would have booked earlier in Q1 are pushing out their bookings (so in a sense, Booking didn't "lose out" on any of this demand). Still, the company noted considerable macro uncertainty as a caveat here that could lead to disappointment.

We find the fact that U.S. airlines are cutting capacity this summer is another weak signal that investors should be aware of. In addition, we're worried about the fact that Booking's growth rates are no longer surpassing peers by a wide margin: Airbnb grew bookings at 7% y/y in Q1 (virtually the same pace as Booking), while Expedia's bookings grew 4% y/y. I continue to view Expedia as the biggest competitive risk, since its integrated OneKey program across all of the company's booking sites offers better benefits and cash back versus Booking's Genius program, which is integrated only into Booking.com (its second-largest subsidiary, Priceline, uses an entirely different rewards system called Priceline VIP, which is a messy experience for consumers in my view).

Looking ahead, Booking continues to focus on its relatively smaller presence in non-hotel bookings (flights and experiences) as a means to bolster its revenue growth. In spite of weaker bookings growth, CEO Glenn Fogel called out particularly strong performance in attractions on the Q1 earnings call:

Much of the work in recent years in building towards the Connected Trip vision has been in standing up the other travel verticals outside of accommodations, like Flights for example. With air ticket growth of 45% in the first quarter, we continue to see strong growth from our flight platform, which is an important component in many of our travelers’ connected trips.

Outside of flights, we are pleased to have seen strong growth in our attractions vertical in the quarter, with attraction tickets increasing 92% year-over-year from a modest base. While the direct financial impact is minimal today, we believe continuing to enhance the attractions vertical allows us to offer our travelers compelling in-destination experiences."

On the profitability front, we do appreciate that Booking continues to maintain tight controls on opex, which has allowed the company to grow adjusted EBITDA by 21% y/y to $1.1 billion.

Trailing twelve-month adjusted EBITDA margins of 35.3% have also improved 170bps y/y, and the company's guidance continues to suggest y/y margin expansion in Q2 and beyond.

Key takeaways

It perhaps goes without saying that I find less attractive opportunity in Booking stock in the mid-$5,000s than I did when it was trading in the low $4,000s, a rally that I don't think Booking has sufficiently justified through fundamental performance. For now, steer clear here and invest elsewhere.

data.title: Blue Owl Capital: Valuation Is Catching Up To Performance For This 10.4%-Yielder
data.description:
In this article, we take a look at the Business Development Company (BDC) Blue Owl Capital Corporation (NYSE:OBDC). In Q1, the company delivered a 2% total NAV return, in line with the median in our coverage and above the average. OBDC trades at a 10.4% total dividend yield and a 10.65% NII yield. We last discussed OBDC here.

OBDC targets borrowers in the upper middle-market space with a weighted average EBITDA of $215m in its portfolio.

Its top sectors are software, healthcare, food/beverage and others.

Quarter Update

Adjusted NII came in at $0.39—well below the $0.47 level in the previous quarter. In our view, this is “payback” from the unusually stable level of NII in the last few quarters despite the drop in short-term rates. Investors may recall that Q4 had $0.05 of non-repeatable income due to accelerated OID or original issue discount and a dividend from a small equity position. Management also pointed to the final pass-through of the Fed's rate cuts and a slowdown in repayment activity as a drag on NII. We expect NII to be relatively stable going forward until the Fed makes its next move.

The base dividend remained the same at $0.37 with a lower $0.01 supplemental. Total dividend coverage was 103% with the base dividend coverage at 105%. Last year, the company adopted a supplemental dividend framework which pays out 50% of the quarterly net excess income above the quarterly dividend. If the Fed delivers on 3-4 rate cuts as the market expects, we would expect the base dividend to be reduced.

The spillover was $0.34—close to one regular dividend.

The NAV fell 0.8%, partly as a result of the Q1 supplemental, as well as the impact of spreads and a few individual positions.

Although the NAV has been on a downtrend in the past year, it's pretty much where it was a few years ago.

On the tariff side, management said that 94% of the portfolio comprises domestic companies that primarily service other domestic, mostly service, customers, limiting the exposure to international trade.

Management also mentioned the low pace of M&A as a headwind. This dynamic is leading to lower lending opportunities, originations, and repayment fees in the sector.

Income Dynamics

Net new investments were slightly lower, in line with the overall subdued level of M&A.

Leverage rose to 1.26x as a result of the merger with OBDE and stands a bit above the 1.15x median level in our coverage and slightly outside of the 0.9-1.25x target range. Management expects leverage to move back into the target range next quarter.

Asset yield fell while interest expense rose, largely as a result of the merger. Both are slightly below median levels in our coverage. Coming into the quarter, the company had two bonds maturing in July of this year with a weighted average coupon of 4.6%, making up just over 6% of the total debt outstanding. The smaller bond was repaid in April, while the larger one was prefunded by tapping the 2029 bond at SOFR + 1.92%. This will slightly raise the company's interest expense. This year, OBDC also reset two CLOs and amended two bilateral SPVs, reducing the interest cost of each.

Lending spreads have stabilized and rose slightly after a steep fall in 2024. The volatility over April likely allowed the company to lend at wider spreads than was the case in Q1.

PIK fell sharply as five names went from partial PIK to fully cash pay. The OBDE merger pulled PIK slightly lower as well, as its portfolio had a lower PIK than the OBDC portfolio. As management discussed a number of times, the vast majority of PIK is structured at inception rather than being an indication of credit issues. That said, a lower PIK is still good to see as it keeps the loan from increasing in size, crowding out other opportunities, and puts less strain on having to raise cash to pay out as dividends.

Portfolio Quality

Non-accruals rose slightly, but remain below the sector median and average levels. Three positions were exited and two were added.

Portfolio quality, as gauged by internal ratings, improved slightly.

There was an unusually large net realized loss during the quarter.

The portfolio allocation to first-lien loans has grown over the last couple of years which is good to see. It is now only slightly below the average in our coverage.

Top 10 investments make up 22% of the portfolio, which is on the high side, and it would be good to see this come lower. The average LTV is just over 40% — a strong level. Interest coverage was 1.8x at today's rates.

Valuation and Returns

OBDC has outperformed the sector in our coverage over the last several years.

It is currently trading slightly richer than its longer-term average valuation.

In relative terms, the valuation is a bit richer due to its historic relationship to the sector.

Despite its less appealing absolute and relative valuation, the combination of its historic performance and valuation remains attractive.

Stance and Takeaways

Coming into the year, OBDC has been one of our biggest overweights across the Income Portfolios. The company has a strong record of generating stable and sector-beating performance. Portfolio quality is holding up and tariffs should not be a big headwind for it. That said, Blue Owl Capital Corporation stock has now made up all of its Liberation Day weakness, which leaves a smaller margin of error for investors.

data.title: 2 Names Delivering Strong Dividend Growth For Passive Long-Term Investors
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman

As an income-focused investor, I tend to incorporate and gravitate toward some higher dividend-yielding names in my portfolio. Of course, not reaching just for the highest paying investments and buying those, as those can often lead to greater risk. However, the other side of this is the more patient, long-term approach of incorporating what might be lower-yielding investments but offer growth over time. For faster-growing companies in terms of earnings and cash flows, this can lead to some significant growth.

This can result in what might be a low yield today, resulting in a substantial yield over time (i.e., yield on cost). Yield on cost tends to be a more fun type of metric rather than a fundamental one that should lead to making a choice on an investment or not, but it does help highlight how your cash flow from original capital can grow meaningfully over time.

Investing in a portion of consistent and reliable dividend growers can help combat the deterioration of your buying power that comes over time due to inflation. Additionally, the passively growing cash flow over time can also negate the negative impact of dividend cutters. Generally, there are some signs that can help avoid dividend cutters that are present, but it can help nonetheless for more passive investors.

Today, I wanted to present two of these dividend growers that I hold, that look set to continue to grow their dividend at a healthy pace.

#1 Mastercard (MA)

Mastercard probably needs little introduction as a well-known household name; most of us have one of their cards in our wallets or purses. When it comes to the investing world, MA is one of those names that most investors probably hold primarily in a growth-oriented portfolio. With a 0.52% yield, it isn't likely to get too many investors excited in the income-focused world. That said, it does get me excited, and one of the reasons I own it is due to its strong 10-year dividend CAGR, coming in at ~18%.

So I'd argue that it is truly a dividend growth name, and the capital appreciation over time is the cherry on top. A $10k investment 10 years ago is now pushing nearly $62k today.

That type of dividend growth is what leads to a strong yield on cost metric as well for an investor. An investor who put money to work here 10 years ago may have around a 0.50% yield at the time, but that would have grown to 3.3% today — 6.6x the yield that one originally started with.

Looking back, over the last ten years, MA only saw earnings decline once, and that was in fiscal year 2020. We all know what was going on around that time, resulting in a truly black swan event. As MA earns a large portion of its revenue from cross-border transactions and limited travel happening during Covid, this decline makes sense. It did not take long for the company's earnings to bounce back, though, as the following year showed record earnings once again.

Analysts continue to expect strong growth in terms of earnings going forward as well, with fiscal 2025 expecting nearly 18% EPS growth, which is double the EPS delivered in FY 2021, its bounce-back year from Covid. With those sorts of figures and a payout ratio of less than 19%, the future continues to look bright on the dividend front for this one.

Even better, the stock doesn't look overly expensive either relative to its historical fair value range. However, the P/E multiple itself being almost 34x is expensive relative to the market and the market's longer-term historical level. With the type of growth that MA has and is expected to deliver, there can be a big premium assigned to these sorts of businesses.

Of course, it also means they have to be executing perfectly. We've seen that more recently with UnitedHealth Group (UNH), which had also been a very consistent business, delivering double-digit growth regularly. And now that there is an expectation for EPS declines this year, the stock has taken a dive. That wasn't the only bad news, either, as there have been a number of rumors related to potential fraud.

#2 Primerica (PRI)

Primerica isn't generally going to be considered as well-known as a company like MA. PRI comes with a $9.15 billion market cap, making it a mid-cap stock. MA is a behemoth at almost $528 billion — firmly placing MA in large-cap market territory. However, as MA was reclassified to the financial sector from the technology sector a few years ago, this puts it in the same sector as PRI.

Except they are in quite different industries within the sector, PRI is a life and health insurance company, primarily focused on term life insurance sales. The company was spun off in 2010 from Citigroup (C). Along with term life insurance, the company also offers an investment and savings product, which has also been helping to drive sales growth. They operate under the model of recruiting independent contractors, which some folks have been critical of, as multi-level marketing strategies can be associated with pyramid schemes.

When it comes to the dividend yield, PRI offers a higher yield compared to MA at 1.49%. That said, most investors still won't consider that a very attractive yield on its own. That's where the monster CAGR dividend growth rate of 21.31% makes it more attractive as an income investment. Last year, they even bumped the payout twice rather than the standard annual raises that it generally goes with.

Looking at the YOC, over the last decade, we would have started at around 1.5%, and today it would be 9.41%—which results in a 6.27x increase during this period. Similar to MA, we would have seen a $10k investment 10 years ago multiply handsomely, moving up to nearly $60k today as well.

Naturally, YOC and growth of $10k in the past are impressive, but that doesn't tell us what the future might hold and if it is worth an investment right now. Looking forward at PRI, the company isn't expected to grow as richly as MA, but EPS growth is still expected to be reasonable, regardless. It just appears as though it is going to slow down compared to what we've seen over the last decade if we average it out.

PRI also only had one year showing EPS declines, and that was in 2022, before seeing a massive rebound the next year.

The payout ratio for the company sits at almost 19%, making it a rather comfortable buffer than it had before worrying too much about overpaying.

Additionally, even if PRI isn't looking to grow as quickly as MA, it also isn't priced for double-digit growth either. It has a modest 13.31x forward P/E and trades on the lower end of its fair value range.

The share price has been moving sideways and struggling for the last six months or so now, while earnings have been continuing to grow. That's what has pushed us from what looked like it was overvalued to being quite undervalued and then back into its fair value channel. In hindsight, this is one of those names that I was considering adding to during the market downturn of April this year, but ultimately pulled the trigger on some other names.

data.title: Urbana Corporation: A Financial-Focused NAV Discount Idea
data.description:
Urbana Corporation (TSX:URB.A:CA, OTCPK:UBAAF, OTC:URNAF) is a pretty straightforward holding company discount case with around a 44% discount to NAV, not very different from something like Treasure ASA which we discussed some time ago on our IG, but with a more diverse portfolio. They have a public and private portfolio. The public portfolio is inoffensive (although we'll note a macro risk regarding the USD and capital market conditions) and by covering Urbana market cap ends up valuing the private portfolio too conservatively even given the coolness of private market activity currently. Liquidity of the Urbana listing isn't too much of a problem but do look into it depending on sizing (gets to $100k USD in daily value) about the URB.A listing (FIGI:BBG000G6RMM6), and we see several private assets that are likely holding their stated values given the activity of public comps, despite the pressures in private markets. So we have some confidence in the NAV discount. Although this company is controlled by the Caldwell Family, so some discount would be appropriate for corporate governance reasons.

NAV

Let's focus on a couple of investments within the private portfolio where we'd be more confident with valuations, and which have a big bearing on the NAV. They have shares in the common stock of the Canadian Securities Exchange, the CNSX, at around 20% of the overall NAV calculation. That would ordinarily be a matter of concern since private markets have gone gelid since then (with few private transactions occurring to benchmark their portfolio) but the performance of those sorts of stocks on public markets has us more confident of valuations.

We note that since last disclosures, there has been around a 25% upward revision in the valuation of CNSX. They also have a stake in the Miami Options Exchange, listed under Miami International Holdings or MIAX which has been revised since last year to a similar degree. We are confident in these revaluations as they reflect growth in public peers to a similar effect, thinking about Intercontinental Exchange, Inc. (ICE) and others.

The other element worth calling out is Blue Ocean Technologies. The TSE completed an investment in BOT which helps establish the company's value. This occurred in late 2023, so there's no reason to question valuations too much. BOT has also seen around a 25% increase in its valuation as calculated by Urbana in its NAV since the precedent transaction, also a player in the operations of markets in the after-hours.

The public investments similarly focus on the financial services industry. Solid, inoffensive picks, lots of blue chips. Maybe a little too much automatic reliance on some financial stalwarts, and we're not crazy about the KKR & Co. Inc. (KKR) pick because we have considerable concerns around private market portfolios, but it could be worse. This NAV chart is very updated, from 30th of May, so we don't even need to rebuild it to establish the mark-to-market figures.

Bottom Line

The thesis is quite simple. The risks are primarily macro related. Credit events would be an issue for the financial exposures. We think there are market risks around policy direction of the U.S., particularly as it affects the dollar which we explain here. A decline in the dollar's dominance, even if only speculated and not materialized, could really hit capital markets by hitting financial company balance sheets and impacting credit conditions, which is bad for both the public and private portfolios. It impacts assumptions around liquidity management and hedging worries.

Related to macro as well, an important systematic factor is the recovery of investment banking and capital market activity. Dealmaking, consolidation and in particular IPOing matter for the full-service banks as well as for the exchange companies. Volatility in politics and economic order puts a damper on this which is unfortunate. Though the exchanges will benefit overall from volatility in markets thanks to their countercyclical business model. They are quite heavy on exchanges with around 42%+ across the private and public portfolios.

The liquidity could be better too. It gets to $100k USD in daily value exchanged quite frequently, but that could mean some frictions. Inform yourself.

Nonetheless, the public market exposures create a strong margin of safety by covering the market cap, and the private exposures are essentially a freebie. The upside to NAV would be 77% at this point if you take the whole private portfolio proposed valuation at face value. Of course, we have to take some discount away due to the fact that the company is family controlled which always creates a bit of uncertainty for the minority shareholders. Moreover, to access the assets in a liquidation a shareholder gets taxed.

But we think it's a decent exposure and better value than building the same exposure from scratch. An issue is that heavy financial lilt in a moment where we're worried about mounting pressure on the USD, both for the direct FX effects on the investment value but also because of the systemic impact that a major dollar decline could have on the financial system.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Amarin Is A Sell Despite The Large Cash Balance
data.description:
Amarin Corporation plc (NASDAQ:AMRN) is a biotech company that has long been attempting to commercialize drugs based on an active ingredient found in fish oil. Amarin's aim is to improve outcomes for people with cardiovascular disease.

I've followed this company, and its commercialization saga, with interest for many years now. Some science between fish oil and improved health outcomes seems quite robust. It's quite reasonable to think that omega-3 oils, such as what is found in fish, could be beneficial in improving heart disease-related outcomes.

Amarin's product is a component of fish oil, Eicosapentaenoic acid ("EPA"). Rather than relying on over-the-counter nutritional supplements or having to eat more fish in their diets, people could now get the active ingredient in the pharmaceutical form via Amarin's Vascepa. The firm's European drug, Vazkepa, is based on the same underlying compound.

As it would turn out, the clinical trial results were ultimately strong enough that Amarin was able to obtain FDA approval for Vascepa. The drug launched and, at first, generated a decent market impact. The company's revenues peaked at $614 million in 2020.

A Successful Drug Didn't Make For A Successful Investment

Unfortunately, FDA approval and commercial launch would not end up being synonymous with a profitable investment for Amarin's shareholders. It costs a ton of money to develop a drug. Even when you get FDA approval, there's no guarantee that the commercial market for it will ever be large enough to recoup your costs and produce a strong return on investment.

In the case of Amarin specifically, the drug had limited U.S. patent life. As such, generic competition is now eroding Vascepa's market share and revenues. Here are Amarin's annual revenues to date:

While there was a nice run-up there for a few years, Amarin's revenues have now fallen by nearly two-thirds from the peak.

It appears that many doctors are still not convinced of the overwhelming health benefits of prescription fish oil. Even though the drug is approved and available to prescribe to patients, it simply didn't become a mainstream blockbuster in the way that Amarin's shareholders would have hoped.

With the U.S. market never reaching expectations and now faced with generic competition, the company has attempted to pivot. More recently, it has emphasized the European market where it has longer patent protection. However, various commercial launches haven't really taken off yet either.

The company's Q1 2025 European revenues came in at $5.4 million and greatly trail where its U.S. revenues are. Meanwhile, U.S. revenues of $35.7 million in Q1 were down another 26% year-over-year, hit by lower selling prices and reduced sales volumes.

Not surprisingly, Amarin as a company is running sizable losses. It takes money to commercialize, market and distribute a drug on an international scale. There simply aren't enough Vascepa/Vazkepa sales for this to be profitable at current adoption levels. Put another way, Amarin's core product simply isn't strong enough to carry the whole company, at least not right now.

In Q1, the company lost $15.7 million, a significantly larger loss than the $10.0 million it lost in the same period of 2024. The company's total operating expenses now slightly exceed its total product revenues, making for a challenging financial situation, especially as revenues continue to decline.

The Bull Case: Trading Under Cash Value

The upside case for a while now has been that Amarin is trading far below book value:

In fact, it's even trading below cash value. As of March 31st, the company had $282 million in cash and short-term investments while the market cap now is about $250 million. Amarin has no debt, either.

Generally, having a cash pile larger than a company's market cap makes for a decent margin of safety. In addition, that cash could make Amarin an attractive M&A target for a larger suitor. A buyer, even after paying a premium to Amarin's current stock price, would be able to get back most of their purchase price in cold hard cash and also obtain the modest but meaningful revenue stream from Vascepa on top of that.

More broadly speaking, it would make a lot more sense to have Vascepa within a larger drug portfolio. In this way, costs associated with it could be divided across many other niche drugs rather than being all unique to Vascepa.

However, Amarin has a long track record as a public company. On several occasions, you could say that Amarin has made decisions that have arguably not been optimal in terms of maximizing shareholder value.

Specifically, as it relates to today, I'm not sure if the company's continued investments in R&D are likely to generate much return on investment at this point. Amarin is currently spending about $20 million in annual R&D. From my vantage point, however, it seems like the cake is pretty well baked in terms of how doctors are going to perceive Vascepa. Amarin has already had many years to make the case to doctors, and I'm not sure one more marginal piece of clinical data will persuade many healthcare professionals at this point.

Launching Vazkepa in Europe is interesting. If even one of those individual country markets such as Spain takes off, that could be a game-changer for the company. But that seems more like a long shot rather than a high-probability outcome that bulls can bank on, at least with the results so far.

If the company continues running losses, on its present course the company is bound to spend through its cash over the next few years. Amarin admittedly looks cheap today. There's a drug that, while not profitable, is doing about $175 million or so of annual revenues. Throw in the huge cash pile, and it's not implausible to think this would be worth more to a strategic buyer.

But if the company stays the course and continues spending its cash on marketing and more R&D, the margin of safety will gradually disappear.

AMRN Stock Verdict

I've long been interested in this company. I do think there's a real chance that fish oil could meaningfully help people with their health journeys. I had been hopeful that Amarin would deliver stronger trial results and have a more successful product on its hands. Unfortunately, at some point, it's time to admit that while the science looks interesting, the investment case has become greatly tarnished.

There is arguably some sunk cost fallacy in holding on to the stock at this point. It's easy to say that stock is already down so much that it makes no sense to sell. That said, there can be up to 100% additional downside on a stock at any given point in time. And if Amarin remains on its present course with overhead costs exceeding drug revenues, the company seems bound to eat through its cash and end up with little to no residual value for shareholders in due time.

I would be cautious about short selling Amarin stock outright. There is a real risk there given the cash balance. If the company did try to maximize shareholder value through strategic alternatives right here and now, the share price would likely rally.

But if the company keeps on its current path, shares will very likely head lower over the next couple of years. Amarin stock has rallied back to $12.50 after recently trading as low as $8. That could make for a great time to get out of positions in this pocket of relative strength. While I am not looking to short sell AMRN stock outright, I believe a sell rating is appropriate as the stock is likely to produce negative returns over the next few years unless there is strategic action before then.

data.title: United Therapeutics: A Cheap But Complicated Tale
data.description:
Today, I put mid-cap biopharma concern United Therapeutics Corporation (UTHR) in the spotlight. A competitor just launched a competing product in the company's core market after years of litigation. In addition, the company posted quarterly results in late April that beat both top and bottom-line expectations. Therefore, it seems a good time to do a deeper dive around United Therapeutics Corporation. An analysis follows below.

United Therapeutics Corporation is headquartered in Silver Spring, MD. The company is focused on developing products to address the unmet medical needs of patients with chronic and life-threatening diseases. The company has more than a half dozen FDA-approved drugs in its product portfolio. The number one contributor to overall sales is Tyvaso DPI, which is an inhaled treprostinil inhalation powder via pre-filled and single-use cartridges to treat pulmonary arterial hypertension or PAH. PAH is the core focus area of United Therapeutics. The stock trades around $325.00 a share and sports an approximate market capitalization of $14.6 billion.

In late May, Liquidia Corporation (LQDA) after a long litigation battle that spanned got the FDA greenlight to officially launch Yutrepia, which will be a direct competitor to the United's Tyvaso franchise. A recent article on Seeking Alpha noted that Yutrepia is expected to be priced significantly under the roughly $300,000 annually for Tyvaso. Other key drugs in United's product portfolio include Remodulin, Orenitram, and Unituxin. Outside of Unituxin, the company's product portfolio is built around Treprostinil.

The company also has several candidates in its pipeline as can be seen above. United Therapeutics is trying to position Tyvaso to treat idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis, where it would have Orphan Drug exclusivity. The company has two pivotal trials enrolled targeting this indication as you can see below. This will open up a significant new market (100,000 individuals) if eventually approved.

In addition, the company is enrolling in a pivotal study evaluating Tyvaso to treat Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis.

United Therapeutics is also trying to position another compound Ralinepag as part of a triple combination to provide a once-a-day pill for PAH. It is currently enrolling a pivotal study for that effort.

Finally, the company also has programs to develop organ alternatives. This is a completely different niche than its established products and is years away from any significant revenue potential.

Recent Results:

United Therapeutics posted its Q1 numbers on April 30th. The company delivered GAAP earnings of $6.63 a share, 35 cents a share north of the consensus. This was up from $6.17 a share in the same period a year ago as net income increased five percent to $322.2 million. Total R&D expenses rose approximately four percent to $105.5 million. Marketing and G&A expenses rose 18% from the same period a year ago, in line with revenue growth.

Revenues rose just over 17% to $794.4 million, more than $65 million better than expectations. It was the 11th straight quarter of double-digit sales growth. Revenues were driven primarily by the Tyvaso franchise with total sales up 25% to $466 million. Revenues were primarily driven by higher volume with a smaller contribution from higher prices on average. United Therapeutics also got solid growth from Remodulin and Orenitram while sales from Unituxin were flat.

Analyst Commentary & Balance Sheet:

The analyst firm community leans positive on United Therapeutics Corporation at the moment. Since first quarter results hit the wires, seven analyst firms including Leerink Partners, Jefferies and JP Morgan have reissued Buy ratings on the stock. Price targets proffered range from $350 to $432 a share. Wells Fargo ($314 price target), Bank of America ($321 price target, up from $314 previously) and Morgan Stanley ($348 price target) maintained Hold ratings on UTHR.

Just under five percent of the outstanding float in the shares is currently held short. Several insiders have been frequent and consistent sellers of the shares for many quarters. Since first quarter numbers were posted, they have disposed of just over $14 million worth of equity collectively. United Therapeutics listed approximately $5 billion in cash and marketable securities on its balance sheet against $200 million against a line of credit facility on the 10-K it filed for the quarter.

Conclusion:

United Therapeutics Corporation made $24.64 a share (GAAP) on $2.88 billion in FY2024. The current analyst firm consensus has profits increasing to $27.68 a share as sales climb to $3.22 billion in FY2025. They project earnings will nudge up to $28.42 a share on revenue growth of six percent in FY2026.

UTHR is trading at under 12 times forward earnings, about half the valuation of the S&P 500 (SP500). The stock is quite a bit cheaper equating to the large amount of net cash on its balance sheet. Given this it is a bit surprising to see the level of insider selling as well as the mixed view on the stock in the analyst firm community and relatively low price targets by the bulls on the stock. A good part of this is due to patent expirations around treprostinil.

There are several other open questions about United Therapeutics. First, how big is Yutrepia to Tyvaso's current franchise? In addition, will United be able to extend Tyvaso to additional indications? What will do United with its huge cash hoard. Buy back more stock? Does it execute some strategic acquisitions to build out its product portfolio?

If management executes against its plans for long-term growth, the stock is more than cheap at current trading levels. In summary, United Therapeutics is a cheap but complicated tale. If the shares fall closer to the $300 level, I will probably take a small position in the stock using covered call orders. Before I would consider a larger position, I would want to see what the potential impacts are from Yutrepia over the next couple of quarters.

data.title: Currency Risks, Part 2: Currency Hedging Of International Equities
data.description:
By Mark Barnes, PhD, & Indhu Raghavan, CFA

Introduction

The recent large moves in the US dollar have brought the idea of currency hedging to investor attention. Currency hedging is often ignored when discussing international equity investments but this recent period of unusual currency moves (discussed in another insight) reminds us of the impact of currency risk. In this insight, we give a gentle review of currency hedging, focusing on hedging equity exposures.

This note discusses the intuition of currency hedging (or simply “hedging” in this article) and does not get into the specifics of hedging costs. Hedging relies on a market for currency forwards that are traded to remove the impact of currency moves, which means that only markets with liquid forwards will allow currency hedging at reasonable prices, and for some markets (e.g. some emerging markets) there is no practical way to hedge currency movements.

In this note we show that the impact of currency hedging on volatility depends on the correlation of the investment country’s equity and currency returns, and so can vary by country. In general, this correlation is positive, so currency hedging reduces the volatility of an international equity investment. However, the US and Japan have negative correlations between their equity and currency returns, and so currency hedging investments in those countries would be expected to increase investment return volatility.

The interaction between equity and currency returns

Currency hedging of equity investment, as is implied by the term, is an action taken to reduce the risk of making an international equity investment. Investors invest in equities because they believe that the equity investment will appreciate in value over time. However, if the investment is an international investment in a country with a different currency, then the return on the equity investment must be converted back into the investor’s home currency when the return is realized, and that means that there is an additional currency return due to the movement in exchange rate with the home currency. This additional currency return can increase or reduce the total home currency return, depending on the direction of the currency return.

Exhibits 1 and 2 show hypothetical examples where the foreign currency moves in the same direction as the equity return and in the opposite direction as the equity return, respectively.

Exhibit 1: Hypothetical equity, currency and total returns - currency return is in the same direction of the equity return

Exhibit 2: Hypothetical equity, currency and total returns - currency return is in a different direction from the equity return

While these examples are hypothetical one-period returns, what is important is the relative size of equity and currency returns over time. We can look at the volatility of returns to get an idea of the relative magnitude of these returns over time. Exhibit 3 show the 10-year volatility (annualized standard deviations of return) for select equities and currencies.

Exhibit 3: Equity and currency annualized standard deviation of returns, selected countries, 10 years through 2025-04-30

In general, currency volatility is lower than equity volatility everywhere, but it is much higher in some countries. All of the currency returns in this insight use the USD exchange rate (e.g. Euro to USD exchange) except for the USD, which uses the DXY trade-weighted dollar index.

When thinking about taking on equity and currency risk, it should be noted that while equities (and fixed income) do have a risk premium and are expected to have a long-term payoff, currencies do not and so they just add expected volatility. Exhibit 4 shows annualized equity and currency returns over the last 10 years. In all cases, we see that equity returns have been positive, but currency returns have ranged from slightly positive in the US and Eurozone to very negative in Turkey, which has gone through a period of high inflation. The point is that long-term currency investment adds expected volatility without adding any expected return.

Exhibit 4: Equity and currency annualized returns, selected countries, 10 years through 2025-04-30

Correlations between currencies and equities

While investing in currency does imply taking on risk, the impact of adding currency exposure to an equity exposure depends on the correlation of the equity and currency returns. Exhibit 1 above gives an example in which the two returns move together, or have a positive correlation, and we would expect this would increase the volatility of the combined equity-currency position. However, if the correlations are negative, as shown in Exhibit 2, currency exposures could reduce overall volatility since the currency move would be expected to dampen the equity returns, both positive and negative.

Exhibit 5 shows the correlation of selected country equity index returns with their currency returns over the 10 years ending 30 April 2025, sorted by correlation. We can divide these countries up into several groups, with perhaps a few leftovers:

  • The high correlation countries are dominated by emerging countries or countries that are primarily resource exporters. It is likely that these countries’ equity markets and currencies are dominated by flows into the country. The marginal investor is a foreign investor that invests in the equity markets (and the currency) when prospects for the country look good and pulls money out when prospects deteriorate.
  • The positive but low correlation countries here are dominated by the Eurozone countries (plus the UK) that have a positive but loose connection between flows into that country’s equity market and flows into the currency. In the case of the Eurozone, because it is a multi-country currency block, equity markets are quite diverse and so currency impacts are muted because equity flows are averaged across many countries.
  • Finally, the only countries here that have negative correlations are Japan and the US. In the case of Japan, the negative correlation has long been observed, and it may be that the marginal equity investor is Japanese who understands that a depreciating currency helps the terms of trade for Japanese exporters and so bids up the equity market when that happens. Finally, US government bonds have traditionally been considered safe-haven assets, and when global risk aversion rises, there are flows into US assets and the US dollar by investors globally. However, during the same period of risk aversion, domestic investors often pull money out of the equity market and invest in bonds, which creates the negative correlation between US equity and currency returns.

Exhibit 5: Correlation of equity to currency returns, 10 years through 2025-04-30

Exhibit 6: Equity-currency 10-year correlations vs correlation effect on volatility

Impact on investments

The expected impact of currency hedging on investments, then, depends on a couple of factors:

  • The volatility of the currency and equity returns at the location of the investment.
  • The correlation of the investment currency return to that of the equity return.

This means the impacts of currency hedging are not general but depend on the location of the investor and the investment. For example,

  • US investors investing in a developed resource exporter such as Norway will see increased volatility of an unhedged investment and so could reduce their return volatility by hedging. They may consider hedging the currency exposure.
  • Similarly, investors in emerging markets will generally see considerably higher volatility with unhedged investments, but hedging emerging currencies may be prohibitively expensive, and so they may have to consider the investment as all-in-one when making investment decisions.
  • US investors investing in Eurozone equities can expect to see only a small reduction in volatility if they hedge because the correlation of the Euro with each of the Eurozone’s country indices is quite low.
  • Foreign investors investing in the US or Japan, however, generally expect to see lower volatility without hedging and may be happy to forgo hedging. This also applies to investments that are dominated by US equities such as global cap-weighted equity indices.

In addition, there may be investors that have a particular view on the direction of a specific currency, in which case they could hedge opportunistically to pick up the currency impact on an equity portfolio. However, there may be more efficient vehicles for investing directly in currencies.

Finally, the discussion here is based on long-term correlations, average returns, and volatility. Currencies can move sharply and deviate from expected patterns, particularly in the short run. Foregoing hedging allows those shocks into the portfolio, whereas hedging removes the currency risk and confines the risk to the intended equity portfolio risk. For example, while the US has had a negative correlation between its currency and equity returns, recently the correlation has been positive, and so currency hedging would have reduced the volatility of those investments.

data.title: HA Sustainable Infrastructure: Great Potential, But Trump May Upset The Cart
data.description:
Introduction

HA Sustainable Infrastructure Capital, Inc. (NYSE:HASI) recently reported its Q1 numbers, so I thought I’d go through them in more detail and give some comments on the outlook, the negatives, and the positives. I think the growth may be sustainable despite the U.S. not playing ball in the green energy transition right now. However, since I am not yet invested in the company, I will be holding off for now.

By the Numbers

Revenues for the quarter came in at around $97m, down -8.4% y/y. Looking at the revenue breakdown in more detail, interest income came in at $66.4m, which is down -3% y/y. Gain on sale of assets came in at around $19m, down -35% y/y, securitization asset income came in at $7m, up around 43% y/y and other income came in at $4.8m, up 171% y/y.

Q1 non-GAAP EPS came in at $0.64, essentially in line with consensus. On a GAAP basis, before we include equity method investments, the company did not turn a profit, as its operating income was -$6m. Now, if we do include the investments the company has a decent share in, those companies made HASI around $88m, which is down 44.5% from the same time last year. Nevertheless, the company’s bottom line came in positive at around $58m, a decrease from $124.5m in Q1 last year.

The company’s core operations seem to contribute very little to the company’s performance. The investments in other companies are the ones to will either push HASI into profit or a loss, depending on how well they perform.

In other key metrics, the company has a robust pipeline of $5.5B at the end of Q1, which tells us there is a lot of demand for sustainable projects in the future. Such visibility is appreciated by investors because they maintain confidence in HASI’s ability to grow earnings and dividends in the future.

What about the company’s financial position? The company ended the quarter with around $67m in cash and equivalents and $4.7B in total debt. That is quite the gap, however, this type of structure is rather typical for specialized investment companies in infrastructure and sustainable finance. The company’s business model is asset-heavy and highly leveraged, maximizing returns through the use of debt. So, is this worrisome? The company’s debt-to-equity ratio came in at around 1.9x, which according to the latest 10-Q report, we can see that it is below the board-approved leverage limit of up to 2.5x. The company’s interest income has been covering the interest expenses every quarter, and fixed-rate debt of 95% of total debt helps manage the interest rate risk, so we shouldn’t expect a lot of fluctuation in interest expenses every quarter. To help even more, the company recently increased its revolving credit facility to $1.55B, and it also has a commercial paper program for short-term funding, which helps its liquidity further.

So, is it risky, nonetheless? To an extent. The DE ratio is quite high, so disruptions in credit markets and declines in asset values could affect its operations if cash flows decline and financing becomes more expensive. Some liquidity risk is still there given how low cash reserves are. HASI depends on its ability to access credit facilities to refinance its debt or sell assets to meet obligations. However, that is all just in theory, and so far, the company has managed its debt risk quite well, so I don’t think the financial position is bad.

Comments on the Outlook

So, for the company to perform well in the future, a lot of good has to happen. Most governments are open to green initiatives, providing companies and individuals with credits for certain actions they take. Let’s take a look at what could dampen the positive outlook currently.

It all begins with the current U.S. administration helmed by Trump. Trump is not known for his sustainability mindset. He is quite the opposite. The slogan “drill, baby, drill” tells us everything we need to know about where his heart is when it comes to environmental issues. His administration will be the biggest obstacle that could hurt the progress of sustainability and clean energy. The latest hit to these initiatives comes from the U.S. House, which plans to phase out clean energy tax credits, as well as slash spending on EVs and other renewable energy, and claw back many other climate-related funds. Furthermore, offshore and onshore wind permitting has been put on hold or completely abolished, with the U.S. withdrawing from the Paris Agreement once again.

Because of these anti-climate actions, clean energy manufacturers either closed, canceled, or downsized almost $8B in projects in the first quarter of 2025 due to these Trump rollbacks.

It is hard to know what is going to happen over the remainder of the presidency, but I don’t think it will be very positive for renewables. Trump may not be able to pass all he wants in this sector, but the policy environment remains uncertain.

How is the company going to be able to survive this?

I would say HASI will be quite resilient and will still have growth going forward. Its business model is quite well diversified across utility-scale renewables, solar, storage, and energy efficiency, with a robust pipeline still in play, which means so far, the pain is not there just yet, as the company can find a lot of future investments that can put its performance to new highs. We know that Trump is not a fan of the Inflation Reduction Act, but some analysts noted that HASI has minimal exposure to the Act, which is a positive.

Despite the negative sentiment from the U.S. policies, global clean energy investments are set to hit a massive 3.3T in 2025. $2.2T is going to clean tech, which is double the investment in fossil fuels. I think we often forget that the U.S. is not the only country in the world, and other countries, especially in Europe, are a bit more open to climate change ideas than the current Trump administration, which will help fuel green initiatives further. The U.S. is becoming more of an outlier because countries in Europe and Asia are doubling down on clean energy. And while HASI’s investments are focused on the U.S., the global momentum for renewables could support the future performance of the company. Although that remains to be seen.

So, in my opinion, the company’s dividend is quite secure going forward. At a current dividend yield of 6.75%. The company recently announced its next quarterly dividend to be paid in July. I don’t expect it to be raised if there are some obstacles in terms of the outlook because its payout ratio is already at 69%, so there isn’t a lot of room. It just got raised by half a cent recently to $0.42, so at least for another three quarters, it will remain at that dividend and maybe raise it again by half a cent the next time. The 6.75% dividend rate is quite attractive, and I am sure a lot of people invest in the company purely on just that, however, I believe that the company has some growth potential too.

Closing Comments

So, a lot of HA Sustainable Infrastructure Capital, Inc.’s success hinges on the policies of the Trump administration and whether there will be a lot more rollbacks. So far, many of the tax credits and other incentives are still in place for 2025. However, that could change next year, which provides us with more uncertainty for at least another year. The company’s $5.5B pipeline is certainly going to help ease some investors, as well as the rich dividend; however, for me, I am not too interested in starting a position currently. The Trump administration is too unpredictable, which makes it quite risky to invest in something that is quite the opposite of Trump’s rhetoric about the environment.

data.title: Palantir: Not All AI Tools Are Created Equal
data.description:
PLTR stock: FQ1 and AI initiatives

I last covered Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR) in late April. That article was entitled “Palantir Q1 Preview: Solid Rule Of 40 Score May Continue.” The article provided a preview for its Q1 earnings report (ER). It rated the stock as hold as “I expect its solid rule of 40 score to continue in Q1, a crucial factor for sustaining its high valuation ratios.”

Since then, there have been several new catalysts evolving around this battle-front stock (apologies for the pun). In this follow-up article, I will examine the top 2 catalysts on my radar. The first one is specific to PLTR’s operations. I will review the business updates it reported in the Q1 ER, especially in terms of its AI offerings. The second one is more general and involves the developments of the DOGE (Department of Government Efficiency) since my last writing. After examining these developments, I keep seeing PLTR as a market leader – or even a market definer—for both commercial and government organizations to leverage AI products. Hence, this article reiterates my hold rating despite the extreme valuation multiples (with an FWD P/E of over 200x as of this writing).

Let me start with the business updates provided in its Q1 ER. As seen, the company continued to deliver growth surpassing consensus estimates (which are already aggressive to start with in my view). In the ER, PLTR provides updates on its AI offerings and product developments. My overall assessment from these updates can be summarized into these 7 words: not all AI products are created equal, and I will elaborate further next.

PLTR stock: not all AI tools are created equal

Under the AI fanfare, many companies are announcing AI initiatives. However, the AI tools that many of them rely on are often ChatGPT. I do see some value in ChatGPT’s general AI tools. However, I believe Palantir’s tools offer truly differentiated value for its customers based on the updates provided in the ER. The ER has provided numerous examples to showcase how PLTR’s tools are outcome-focused and can drive tangible results for its customers. For example, with PLTR tools, Heineken is transforming its supply chain using AI agents and their team built what used to take 3 years before in 3 months.

I expect the differentiated tools to further augment the speed and scale at which PLTR can deploy its products and convert customers going forward. The results provided in Q1 seem to support this expectation. As you can see from the ER slide below, Q1 saw 71% Y/Y growth and 19% Q/Q growth in U.S. commercial revenue growth Customer counts growth at very similar paces. Finally, note the 2x Y/Y growth of deals closed worth $1 M or more. These robust growth rates clearly reflect the effectiveness of both PLTR’s AI technologies and also its marketing approaches, in my view.

Before leaving this section, let me point out another timely tool that PLTR has in its pipeline. PLTR also rolled out a tariff management application recently. Given the uncertainties on this front since “Liberation Day,” I anticipate new products like this to keep attracting new customers to PLTR.

PLTR stock: my previous DOGE concern

Now, I will switch to the second catalyst – the changes in the broader geopolitical landscape. As mentioned in my last article, one concern I have over PTLR is its heavy reliance on U.S. government contracts. With efforts the DOGE was making at that time to trim government spending, I felt this risk was heightened at that time. However, the developments of the DOGE in the past 2 months or so have encouraged me to reassess this risk and I believe it is much lower than what I previously feared.

In the meantime, other developments have led me to see Palantir well positioned to benefit from the policy changes under the Trump administration. For example, recently, President Trump signed an executive order to modernize defense acquisitions. This EO made it clear the Department of Defense, a key contractor for PLTR, will pursue commercial-like contract terms and apply a “first preference for commercial solutions” going forward. I expect this EO to change the approach of many other defense contractors in their bidding of defense contracts, thus opening up new opportunities for PLTR given its unique strengths. Besides its leading and differentiated AI tools as aforementioned, another key strength for PLTR in my view is its integrated software and hardware efforts. Its TITAN program (see details from its investor presentation below) is a good example of this integrated approach.

Risks and final thoughts

In terms of downside risks, Palantir's high valuation remains a cause of concern in my model. It’s current P/E of 206x as you can see from the following table, borderlines absurd levels in absolute terms. Even when compared to other high-growth AI companies, this is extreme and stands out as an outlier. Also, I mentioned that my previous analysis seemed to have overestimated the impacts of DOGE. But in the overall scheme of things, PLTR’s current revenue mix is still heavily relying on a relatively small number of clients, especially government customers. For example, according to this USB analysis, about 55% of Palantir's current revenue is derived from contracts with the U.S. federal government.

With such concentration, PLTR could be particularly sensitive to changes in its competitors’ offerings and technological advancements. And PLTR does face intense competition from both established tech giants and emerging startups, both for commercial and government contracts. The list of competitors ranges from familiar tech names such as Microsoft (MSFT) (in particular its Azure’s Government-specific offerings) and Amazon Web Services (AMZN) to more recent entrants such as Anthropic (whose "Claude Gov" models are directly targeting government AI contracts). Defense contractors such as Lockheed Martin (LMT), Boeing (BA), and RTX Corporation (RTX) have traditionally focused on hardware developments. However, these large defense contractors are now increasingly investing in advanced software, AI, and data analytics in their offerings for government projects. As such, PLTR must constantly invest heavily in research and development, and it remains to be seen if it can hold its competitive moat in the longer term.

To conclude, this follow-up article on PLTR has been triggered by 2 catalysts since my last writing: the business updates reported in the Q1 ER and the developments of the DOGE. These developments have led me to continue viewing PLTR as a leader – or even definer – in AI products. In particular, I expect the company to enjoy new opportunities with defense contracts given the anticipated change in the bidding approach, its differentiated AI tools, and also its integration of software and hardware. Based on these considerations, I maintain a hold rating despite the extreme P/E ratios.

data.title: Victoria's Secret Belongs Behind Closed Doors
data.description:
By Breakingviews

Victoria’s Secret (VSCO) is struggling with the public glare. Since becoming independent in 2021, the lingerie retailer’s shares have halved. An Australian billionaire who amassed a 13% stake has sparked defensive tactics, but it’s an opportunity for boss Hillary Super to initiate a search for a buyer keen to remake the company in private.

The 48-year-old chain, which gained fame by featuring supermodels in its catalogs and TV ads, had been housed within L Brands. A series of missteps attracted the attention of pushy investor Barington Capital, which successfully urged a separation from soap seller Bath & Body Works (BBWI). Neither company has benefited from the split.

Efforts to create a leaner Victoria’s Secret worked, but they have failed to impress investors. It achieved a three-year cost-cutting goal of more than $250 million ahead of schedule. And yet its $7.4 billion sales goal for 2026 is considered too ambitious, based on the $6.3 billion penciled in by analysts, according to Visible Alpha. A cybersecurity breach disclosed on Tuesday, which shut down corporate systems last month and is expected to hit future quarterly results, won’t help either.

Super, who was named CEO last August, is trying to recharge growth, including by bringing back sports-bra brand VSX. It will be difficult to recapture the former glory at Victoria’s Secret, however. Its market share in U.S. women’s underwear tumbled to 18% last year from almost 27% in 2019, per Euromonitor data.

At this stage, it would be better to lean into the outside interest. Victoria’s Secret adopted a poison pill, or the ability to flood the market with shares, in response to the unwanted stake accumulation by Brett Blundy’s Sydney-based investment shop BBRC, accusing the buyer of trying to gain control without paying a premium.

Victoria’s Secret should interest others, too. If a buyout firm paid a 30% premium, it would value the equity at about $2.4 billion. With less than $600 million of net debt and roughly the same amount of EBITDA, there’s also room to borrow to fund an acquisition.

Assume a new owner could lift sales by 2% a year and the operating margin by 1 percentage point, to 10%. At the same 5 times EBITDA valuation in five years, Victoria’s Secret would generate a 24% return, according to Breakingviews calculations. Like lacy undergarments, a company is sometimes better appreciated only after it has been kept under wraps.

Context News

  • Victoria’s Secret said on June 3 that it had temporarily shut down its website and corporate systems on May 26 after detecting a cybersecurity incident. The website was restored on May 29.
  • The lingerie retailer added that it would postpone the release of complete financial results for the first quarter ended May 3, originally slated for June 5. It said it is expecting net sales of $1.4 billion and $32 million in adjusted operating income, and that the breach will affect future results.
  • Victoria’s Secret adopted a shareholder rights plan on May 20 after Australian billionaire Brett Blundy’s investment firm increased its stake. As of April, BBRC holds about 10.3 million shares, or about 13% of the lingerie maker.
  • The so-called poison pill was put in place to “guard against tactics to gain control of the company without paying all shareholders an appropriate premium for that control,” Victoria’s Secret Chair Donna James said.
  • Under the plan, the company said it would issue one right per share on May 29, which would become active if a shareholder acquired at least a 15% stake.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
